coredata.dc:description: Chronic wound management remains a critical challenge in global healthcare, driving the exploration of advanced biomaterials such as hydrogels and carbon dots (CDs). Hydrogels provide a moist microenvironment conducive to cellular proliferation and tissue regeneration, while CDs offer inherent antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties to combat infection and accelerate healing. However, the clinical translation of these materials is hindered by intrinsic limitations: hydrogels often exhibit inadequate mechanical strength for exudate management and controlled drug release, whereas CDs suffer from aqueous instability and aggregation-induced quenching. To overcome these constraints, emerging research focuses on integrating CDs into hydrogel networks. This approach has emerged as a synergistic strategy that not only enhances mechanical robustness and prevents CD aggregation/oxidation but also enables spatiotemporal therapeutic delivery. This review systematically explores (1) innovative synthesis strategies (e.g., non-covalent/covalent crosslinking), (2) the multifunctional properties (mechanical strength, drug-loading capacity, and stimuli-responsive behaviors), and (3) their stage-specific roles in wound healing: achieving immediate hemostasis via rapid fluid absorption capacity, modulating inflammatory phases through ROS scavenging, promoting proliferative regeneration via sustained growth factor release, and enabling tissue remodeling through ECM-mimicking architectures. By bridging material science with clinical demands, this work establishes design principles for next-generation CD-hydrogels and outlines their transformative potential in precision wound care.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chronic wound management remains a critical challenge in global healthcare, driving the exploration of advanced biomaterials such as hydrogels and carbon dots (CDs). Hydrogels provide a moist microenvironment conducive to cellular proliferation and tissue regeneration, while CDs offer inherent antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties to combat infection and accelerate healing. However, the clinical translation of these materials is hindered by intrinsic limitations: hydrogels often exhibit inadequate mechanical strength for exudate management and controlled drug release, whereas CDs suffer from aqueous instability and aggregation-induced quenching. To overcome these constraints, emerging research focuses on integrating CDs into hydrogel networks. This approach has emerged as a synergistic strategy that not only enhances mechanical robustness and prevents CD aggregation/oxidation but also enables spatiotemporal therapeutic delivery. This review systematically explores (1) innovative synthesis strategies (e.g., non-covalent/covalent crosslinking), (2) the multifunctional properties (mechanical strength, drug-loading capacity, and stimuli-responsive behaviors), and (3) their stage-specific roles in wound healing: achieving immediate hemostasis via rapid fluid absorption capacity, modulating inflammatory phases through ROS scavenging, promoting proliferative regeneration via sustained growth factor release, and enabling tissue remodeling through ECM-mimicking architectures. By bridging material science with clinical demands, this work establishes design principles for next-generation CD-hydrogels and outlines their transformative potential in precision wound care.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chronic wounds, such as diabetic foot ulcers [1,2], present a significant global healthcare challenge due to their slow healing, susceptibility to bacterial infections [3], high recurrence rates, and severe complications like cellulitis and abscesses [4], which impose substantial physical, psychological, and economic burdens on patients. Traditional wound dressings, including gauze, plaster, and bandages, while effective in exudate absorption and wound protection [3,5], lack essential bioactivity, such as anti-inflammatory properties and antibacterial [6], and may cause secondary injuries due to adhesion [7,8]. Consequently, research has shifted toward developing advanced wound dressings to optimize the wound microenvironment and accelerate healing.

Hydrogels, hydrophilic polymeric materials with excellent biocompatibility [9,10], have been widely used in biomedical applications [11], including drug delivery [12–14], biosensing [15] and tissue regeneration. While most hydrogels exhibit low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, their potential toxicity primarily stems from material composition and properties. By selecting natural polymers as the primary raw materials during hydrogel preparation, the potential toxicity of hydrogels can be significantly mitigated, thereby enhancing their safety and applicability in biomedical applications. However, hydrogels often suffer from poor mechanical properties, such as loose cross-linking, low solid content, and structural inhomogeneity [16], limiting their effectiveness in sustained wound protection, exudate absorption, and controlled drug release. Strategies to enhance mechanical strength, such as adding small-molecule cross-linkers or initiators [17–19], often introduce biotoxicity [20] and uneven internal structures [21], reducing viscoelasticity [22], thus necessitating non-toxic alternatives.

Carbon dots (CDs), as zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, can be categorized into the following types: carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [23]. Among these, CQDs exhibit a spherical morphology with small dimensions, featuring distinct lattice structures and chemical functional groups on their surfaces, along with sp2/sp3 hybridized crystalline cores. CNDs possess a higher degree of carbonization, with some chemical groups on their surfaces but no apparent lattice structure. CPDs, on the other hand, display a polymer/carbon hybrid structure, rich in surface functional groups and polymer chains, while containing a carbon core internally. GQDs consist of tiny fragments of single- or few-layer graphene, with well-defined graphene lattice structures and chemical groups at their edges [24]. CQDs, CNDs, and CPDs are typically synthesized via bottom-up approaches using organic molecules (e.g., biomass and polymers), whereas GQDs are predominantly prepared through top-down methods, such as oxidative reduction cutting or physical grinding [25,26]. This review focuses on hydrogel composites constructed from these four types of CDs.

CDs exhibit antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and pro-healing properties, including macrophage polarization toward the M2 phenotype [27], cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and tissue repair [28,29]. Their antibacterial mechanisms involve oxidative stress induction, cellular structure disruption, and DNA mutation [30], making them effective against drug-resistant bacteria [31–35] and beneficial for infected wound healing [36]. With the exception of a few CDs that have shown some toxicity in vivo [37], the vast majority of reported CDs exhibit low toxicity. However, CDs tend to aggregate in aqueous solutions due to surface functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, aromatic rings) [38,39], reducing dispersion, stability, and antioxidant activity [40], while surface reductive groups are prone to oxidation, degrading optical properties and limiting biomedical applications.

Integrating CDs with hydrogels to form CDs-hydrogel composites addresses these limitations: CDs enhance hydrogel mechanical strength via covalent or non-covalent bonding, while the hydrogel's three-dimensional network inhibits CD aggregation and oxidation [41–44], improving dispersion, stability, and antioxidant retention. This synergistic combination offers a promising strategy for advanced wound care, leveraging the unique properties of both materials to overcome individual shortcomings and enhance therapeutic efficacy. This review comprehensively examines the potential applications of CDs-hydrogels in wound healing, delving into their synthesis mechanisms, functional properties, and therapeutic efficacy. Section 2 outlines the binding interactions between CDs and hydrogel matrices. Section 3 highlights the multifunctional characteristics of CDs-hydrogels. Section 4 further elaborates on the stage-specific applications of CDs-hydrogel composites across wound healing phases. Collectively, this review identifies current challenges in CDs-hydrogel development and proposes future research directions, aiming to provide theoretical foundations for designing next-generation hydrogel systems and advancing innovative therapeutic strategies in wound management (see Scheme 1 ).

Recent advancements in CDs-hydrogel composites have garnered significant attention, with existing reviews predominantly focusing on synthesis methods [45–48], mechanical optimization, and biomedical applications within traditional classification frameworks. However, conventional categorization based on material sources or preparation techniques fails to systematically elucidate the synergistic mechanisms between CDs and hydrogel matrices. This review classifies the construction strategies of CDs-hydrogels into non-covalent interactions (hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, and electrostatic interactions) and covalent crosslinking (amide bonds and Schiff base bonds). It also compares the complementarity of these two strategies. In particular, we focus on analyzing the regulatory role of CDs in the hydrogel network. We also summarize the effects of different bonding types on the swelling behavior, crosslinking density, and drug-controlled release of hydrogels. Through spatial distribution analysis of CDs within hydrogel networks and interfacial interaction characterization, we unveil the pivotal regulatory role of CD surface functional groups in dynamic network reconstruction. Regarding performance evaluation, this review first summarizes mechanical properties (compressive/tensile strength, swelling behavior) and self-healing capabilities (dynamic bond recombination), validating their suitability as wound dressings. In addition, we systematically summarized the mechanical properties of various CDs-hydrogels from 19 studies between 2021 and 2024 in a table to facilitate the comparative assessment of different carbon point hydrogels. Furthermore, by deciphering triple-stimuli (pH/temperature/light) responsive mechanisms, we elucidate the molecular pathways underlying CD-mediated photodynamic and photothermal synergistic bactericidal effects. Through systematic analysis of 35 pivotal studies (2021–2025), this work innovatively proposes a four-stage wound healing adaptation theory: 1) Hemostasis stage via surface charge-modulated coagulation cascade activation; 2) Anti-inflammatory phase through ROS scavenging and M2 macrophage polarization; 3) Proliferation stage by growth factor-controlled release to enhance fibroblast migration; 4) Remodeling phase employing ECM-mimetic structures for collagen orientation. This research provides theoretical frameworks and technical pathways for molecular design and clinical translation of CD-hydrogel systems.

While the therapeutic potential of CDs-hydrogel composites in wound healing has been extensively explored, achieving robust interfacial integration between CDs and hydrogel macromolecular matrices remains a central challenge. Current fabrication strategies can be primarily categorized into non-covalent interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding [49], electrostatic forces [50]) and covalent crosslinking (e.g., amide [51] and Schiff base bonds [52]) based on binding mechanisms.

Hydrogen bonding [57], van der Waals forces [54] and electrostatic attraction [58] are critical non-covalent interactions for constructing CDs-hydrogels, enhancing mechanical properties, structural recovery, and functional performance. These interactions can be formed by the amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups that are abundant in CDs and hydrogels (such as polyacrylic acid, pectin, and sodium alginate) [34,59]. Compared with other non-covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds possess advantages such as moderate strength and dynamic reversibility. Therefore, hydrogen bonding has become the predominant method for integrating CDs with hydrogels, providing the foundational stability for CDs-hydrogels. In the GQD-PAA hydrogel, the –COOH groups of GQDs form hydrogen bonds with the –OH groups of PAA, establishing a foundational stability. van der Waals forces further enhance the interactions between the CDs and the polymer chains, promoting the uniform dispersion of the CDs and thereby improving the overall performance of the hydrogel (Fig. 1 B) [54]. The electrostatic interactions between CDs and the hydrogel network originate from the dissociation of hydrophilic functional groups (-COOH and –NH2) into charged groups (-COO- and –NH3 +). The amino groups in chitosan partially dissociate into positively charged –NH3 + in aqueous solution, while the carboxyl groups in carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC) dissociate to produce negatively charged -COO-. The electrostatic attraction between these two types of ions promotes the combination of chitosan and CMC, enhancing the structure of the hydrogel (Fig. 1C) [55]. Similarly, the -COO- in CDs effectively crosslink with the –OH and –NH2 in chitosan (CS) via hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions. This process enhances the mechanical strength and stability of the chitosan nanocomposite hydrogel films (Fig. 1D). [56]. These non-covalent strategies collectively advance CDs-hydrogel design for biomedical and material applications.

Compared to non-covalent crosslinking, covalent crosslinking endows CDs-hydrogels with enhanced mechanical properties through bonds such as amide and Schiff base linkages [61]. The amide bonds formed by amino and carboxyl groups exhibit high stability. This stability enables them to remain unchanged in acidic and alkaline environments, thereby enhancing the overall mechanical properties of the hydrogel network. The carboxyl groups of curcumin-derived CDs and cellulose nanofibers (CNF) form amide bonds with the amino groups of gelatin and nitrogen-doped CDs. The presence of amide bonds enhances the storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) of the hydrogel. This network structure based on amide bonds endows the material with excellent toughness and elasticity, thereby improving the overall mechanical properties of the hydrogel (Fig. 2 A and B) [51]. However, excessive covalent stability from amide bonds restricts CDs’ release, limiting their penetration and anti-inflammatory efficacy in wounds. To address this, Schiff base bonds (CN), formed between aldehyde and amino groups, offer pH-responsive cleavage in acidic microenvironments, enabling controlled CDs release for deep tissue sterilization and inflammation suppression. The aldehyde groups in modified cellulose nanocrystals (CCHOs) or CS can form Schiff base bonds with the amino groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. 2C and D). These covalent bonds exhibit dynamic reversibility, allowing CDs-hydrogels to undergo reversible cleavage and reformation in response to external forces or environmental changes [63,64]. However, sometimes the Schiff base bond is relatively unstable.

CDs-hydrogels play a pivotal role in wound healing, where their mechanical properties (e.g., tensile strength, compressive modulus [68], swelling behavior [69], and adhesion), self-healing capabilities [52], and drug-loading/releasing performance [70] critically influence tissue regeneration. Superior mechanical and self-healing properties ensure dynamic conformability to wound surfaces, provide physical support to surrounding tissues, and protect against external mechanical stress, while efficient drug-loading/release accelerates healing [71]. CDs enhance hydrogel compressive resilience and deformation adaptability through covalent or non-covalent crosslinking. In covalent systems, CD-Plys hydrogels constructed via Schiff base reactions between aldehyde groups on CD31 surfaces and amino groups of ε-polylysine (Plys) exhibit enhanced mechanical strength and structural integrity during deformation (Fig. 3 A), achieving self-healing within 30 min through dynamic imine bond recombination (Fig. 3B) [72]. However, in non-covalent systems, hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, and electrostatic interactions can significantly enhance the network structure of hydrogels, increase crosslinking density, and strengthen intermolecular forces. Moreover, these interactions can modulate the flexibility of the network, thereby markedly improving the mechanical properties of the hydrogels. The synergistic hydrogen-bonding network among CMC, CQDs, and iodine (I2) significantly enhances the mechanical properties of the hydroge. The compressive strength of this hydrogel reaches 250 kPa, which is significantly higher than that of PVA hydrogel (150 kPa). More importantly, hydrogen bonding endows PAI/CMC/CQDs hydrogels with excellent bending/twisting adaptability (Fig. 3C) [73]. The CG-KCD hydrogel, designed with a hydrogen-bonding/electrostatic dual network, maintains structural stability under 100 % tensile strain and demonstrates adhesion suitable for 180° joint flexion (Fig. 3D and E) [34]. Similarly, hydrogen bonds are formed between the amino groups, carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GCDs and the carboxyl groups of pectin and polyacrylic acid. These hydrogen bonds, together with the van der Waals forces between adjacent GCDs in the system, collectively enhance the rigidity of the GCDs-PAA hydrogel. Compared with PAA, the swelling behavior of GCDs-PAA remains consistent within 8 h but shows a slight downward trend after 16 h. This change allows GCDs-PAA to absorb excess tissue fluid from the wound without excessive swelling. GCDs-PAA exhibits stronger resistance to deformation than PAA hydrogel. At a strain of 4.5 %, the stress of GCDs-PAA hydrogel is approximately 0.25 kPa, while that of PAA hydrogel is only about 0.08 kPa (Fig. 3G–I) [53].

The mechanical properties of CDs-hydrogels are influenced by three primary factors: the type of CDs, the gel matrix, and the crosslinking method. Given the relatively small size of CDs, their impact on the mechanical properties of hydrogels is limited. In contrast, the gel matrix and crosslinking method are the key determinants of the mechanical performance of hydrogels. Therefore, to evaluate the impact of crosslinking methods on the mechanical properties of CDs-hydrogels, it is essential to select the same gel matrix. Based on this, a comparative analysis of the mechanical property parameters of different CDs-hydrogels should be performed (see Table 1 ).

Among the two PVA hydrogels (PVA-PEDOT: PSS/GCDs and BC/PVA/NGO), the former is crosslinked via hydrogen bonds, while the latter involves both hydrogen bonds and ester bonds. The stress and swelling rate of the former (45 kPa, 450 %) are significantly lower than those of the latter (95 kPa, 800 %). This indicates that the synergistic effect of covalent and non-covalent bonds on mechanical property enhancement is superior to that of non-covalent bonds alone.

Similarly, among the two chitosan hydrogels (CS/CQD and TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD), the former is crosslinked via hydrogen bonds and ionic interactions, while the latter is crosslinked through hydrogen bonds and Schiff base bonds. The covalent Schiff base bonds endow the latter with a higher breaking stress of 6.6 MPa, compared to the former's 88.6 ± 3.2 kPa. However, the swelling ratio of the latter (240.6 %) is significantly lower than that of the former (850 %), which may be attributed to the stronger aqueous stability of ionic bonds [74]. It is worth noting that, unlike covalent bonds, the synergistic effect of multiple non-covalent bonds can exert multidimensional impacts on the mechanical properties of CDs-hydrogels.

Among the two sodium alginate hydrogels (sodium alginate/CD and CDs Crosslinked Sodium Alginate), the former is crosslinked via electrostatic interactions, while the latter involves both hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions. The tensile strength of the former (0.207 MPa) is significantly lower than that of the latter (56.74 MPa). However, the elongation at break of the former (40.301 %) is markedly higher than that of the latter (5.13 %). This indicates that the synergistic effect of hydrogen bonds and ionic bonds is beneficial for enhancing tensile strength, whereas the sole reliance on electrostatic interactions is advantageous for improving flexibility and deformation capacity.

CDs-hydrogels exhibit remarkable swelling and hygroscopic properties, enabling efficient adsorption and loading of water-soluble drug molecules. As shown in Fig. 3H, GCDs-PAA hydrogels gradually swell in PBS solution over time, displaying a trend similar to pure PAA hydrogels but with a reduced equilibrium swelling ratio (from 7.12 to 6.21). This reduction is attributed to the decreased pore size caused by GCDs incorporation, which restricts water molecule permeation and diffusion, thereby creating a more stable drug-loading matrix [53]. Similarly, the porous structure of CG-KCD hydrogels facilitates rapid diffusion of rhodamine 6G from loaded regions to blank hydrogel areas within 2 h, demonstrating superior drug-loading capacity. Notably, these hydrogels exhibit excellent injectability, allowing precise formation of gel columns or specific patterns (e.g., ‘AAU’ characters; Fig. 3I), highlighting their potential for targeted drug delivery applications [34].

CDs-hydrogels demonstrate stimuli-responsive characteristics through various crosslinking methods, such as pH [73], temperature, and laser [63,64,75]. Healthy skin maintains a slightly acidic pH (4–6), critical for suppressing pathogenic microorganism proliferation [76], while infected wounds shift to a slightly alkaline microenvironment (pH 7–8), fostering bacterial growth [77–79]. Restoring acidic conditions effectively inhibits bacterial activity [80], driving research into pH-responsive CDs-hydrogels for precise wound monitoring and management. As shown in Fig. 4 A, a TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogel exhibits dual pH-responsive mechanisms. One is protonation-triggered TA release under acidic conditions to neutralize alkaline wounds and inhibit bacteria, and the other is pH-dependent CQD release via Schiff base cleavage (maximal at pH 5.5) for microenvironment monitor [81]. Similarly, Yang et al. further engineered a pH-responsive hydrogel integrating colorimetric sensing and CQD fluorescence to dynamically monitor wound pH dynamics, enhancing smart wound management strategies [73].

The stratum corneum of healthy skin prevents excessive water loss and maintains hydration, but damaged epidermal tissue in wounds disrupts this moisture balance, exposing internal cells to bacterial/viral invasion and delaying healing [82]. To address this, temperature-responsive CDs-hydrogels have been developed to form in situ via body temperature, adhering to irregular wounds, preserving moisture, and enabling controlled drug release. Guo et al. [83] engineered a PTPH-SCDs hydrogel using mPEG12, PTMCn, and spermine-o-phenylenediamine carbon dots (SCDs), where rising temperature (4 °C→37 °C) triggers mPEG12 dehydration and PTMC hydrophobic aggregation, inducing sol-gel transition for in situ wound adhesion and moisture retention (Fig. 4B). Similarly, Wang et al. [84] designed an AA-CDs@Gel hydrogel by optimizing Pluronic F127 ratios, enabling uniform in situ gelation at 37 °C with photothermal antibacterial and antioxidant functions.

The treatment of drug-resistant bacterial infections during wound healing presents a critical challenge, as antibiotic overuse risks exacerbating microbial resistance [85]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [86] have emerged as resistance-free alternatives, leveraging photoactive nanomaterials to convert light energy into heat or reactive oxygen species (ROS) for broad-spectrum bactericidal effects [87]. Carbon dots (CDs), with intrinsic photothermal and photodynamic properties, enable the construction of photo-responsive CDs-hydrogels for combating drug-resistant pathogens. For instance, Zheng et al. [88] developed a GOQDs@SF/CS (GSC) hydrogel by integrating silk fibroin (SF), chondroitin sulfate (CS), and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), where the GSC10 hydrogel achieved a photothermal conversion efficiency of 48.49 % (Fig. 4C). Similarly, Lee et al. [89] engineered a CDs-hydrogel (Cdgel) that releases CDs under light irradiation. These CDs transfer energy to oxygen via radiative dissipation, generating cytotoxic singlet oxygen (1O2) to eradicate drug-resistant bacteria and enhance infected wound repair.

Wound healing involves four phases including hemostasis, inflammation resolution [96], proliferation [97], and remodeling [47], however complications like bacterial infection and chronic diseases often impede this process [98]. CDs-hydrogels have emerged as multifunctional platforms to address these challenges across healing stages [99] (Table 2 ).

Hemostasis is the initial stage of wound healing. Delayed hemostasis can lead to continuous bleeding of the wound, increasing the risk of infection, and even triggering multiple organ dysfunction syndrome and death [100]. During hemostasis, the high swelling capacity of CDs-hydrogels enables exudate absorption and physical barrier formation to accelerate coagulation. A SOD-activated hydrogel (SOD-AO, composed of CS-His/OHA/CQD) electrostatically adsorbs negatively charged red blood cells (RBCs), inducing RBC aggregation and coagulation factor activation. Concurrent hydrogel swelling seals bleeding sites, achieving rapid hemostasis with low blood clotting index (BCI), enhanced RBC adhesion/aggregation, minimal blood loss, and accelerated clotting kinetics (Fig. 5 A) [95]. Similarly, the TA/QCMCS/OSA@CQD hydrogel demonstrates excellent hemostatic performance due to the positively charged quaternary ammonium groups in QCMCS, which promote platelet aggregation and accelerate the hemostatic process (Fig. 5B) [81].

Bacterial infection is a primary contributor to wound inflammation, and antimicrobial therapy serves as a critical strategy for inflammation mitigation [101,102]. CDs-hydrogels exert anti-inflammatory effects through three key mechanisms. First, their bactericidal activity relies on photothermal effects. Studies demonstrated that CG-KCDs hydrogels under laser irradiation disrupt bacterial morphology and membrane permeability, triggering β-galactosidase release and ONPG hydrolysis to degrade cell walls (Fig. 5C), with significantly enhanced antibacterial efficacy against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus (OD values surpassing control groups) [34]. Near-infrared photothermal hydrogels reduced bacterial survival rates to 6.95 % ± 0.43 % and 7.17 % ± 0.62 % [103], while other photothermal-responsive hydrogels exhibited similar antibacterial performance [88,104]. It is important to note that the localized temperature increase of the photothermal effect may cause damage to surrounding healthy tissue. To avoid this, the temperature can be controlled at around 45 °C, a temperature threshold that effectively protects normal tissues from thermal damage. Second, CDs-hydrogels regulate oxidative stress via dual ROS generation and scavenging [105]. CDs generate ROS (e.g., O2 −, 1O2, ·OH) to denature bacterial proteins, disrupt membranes, and damage DNA (Fig. 5D) [6,34], as seen in light-triggered hydrogels releasing 1O2 [96]. Additionally, Wang [106] and Jin et al. [107] also developed CDs-hydrogels that generate ROS through decomposition of H2O2. Conversely, certain CDs-hydrogels, such as LAMC/CD-C@M@P, effectively scavenge ROS (55.29 %, 57.93 %, and 53.67 % elimination rates for H2O2, ·OH, and O2 −, respectively; (Fig. 5E), mitigating oxidative damage [3,108]. Hydrogels incorporating ascorbic acid-derived CDs [84] and green tea phenolic compounds [57] also neutralize ROS to protect cells. Third, CDs-hydrogels alleviate inflammation by suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine activity. Self-healing hydrogels reduced TNF-α and IL-6 expression (Fig. 5F) [109], while additional studies confirmed their ability to modulate immune cell function, oxidative stress, and cellular signaling pathways for inflammation resolution [54,110].

During the proliferative phase of wound healing, CDs-hydrogels have been extensively demonstrated to enhance fibroblast migration and proliferation, critical for tissue repair [93]. In the LAMC/CD-C@M@P hydrogel, a higher Ce3+/Ce4+ ratio significantly accelerates cell migration. The scratch coverage rate of HUVECs in the experimental group reached 65.24 ± 1.13 % within 24 h, which was significantly higher than that of the control group (37.07 ± 2.16 %)(Fig. 6 A) [3]. Additional CDs-hydrogels composites have similarly enhanced cell migration and accelerated skin repair [73,109]. In the tissue remodeling phase, CDs-hydrogels promote angiogenesis [111], collagen deposition [109], and granulation tissue regeneration. After treatment with EPL@poly(NVP-co-NMA)@AAB-CDs, the regenerated epithelial tissue exhibited excellent healing at 12 days (8.31 ± 3.65 %), with a smaller wound area compared to the control group (12.25 % ± 4.56 %) (Fig. 6B) [93]. Similarly, thermosensitive PTPH-SCDs hydrogel reduced wound diameter, decreased inflammatory cells, and increased collagen deposition and mature blood vessel formation [83]. Other CDs-hydrogels systems have also demonstrated efficacy in enhancing tissue remodeling and vascularization [91,102].

This review systematically summarizes the binding mechanisms, response strategies, and applications of CDs-hydrogels in wound healing. Despite their progress in promoting wound repair, CDs-hydrogels still face challenges and opportunities that define future research directions. The following perspectives are proposed.
1.
The construction of CDs-hydrogels often requires small amounts of crosslinking agents, whose potential biotoxicity may hinder wound healing. Future studies should focus on designing CDs with specific functional groups to develop crosslinking agent-free CDs-hydrogels.
2.
Wound healing is a dynamic process, necessitating CDs-hydrogels with real-time monitoring capabilities. However, current systems predominantly rely on single-response strategies, limiting their ability to track multiple changes during healing or address diverse therapeutic demands. Researchers should prioritize integrating diverse responsive materials to create multi-responsive hydrogels for multidimensional monitoring and treatment.
3.
Chronic wounds (e.g., diabetic foot ulcers) exhibit complexity due to external factors (e.g., hyperglycemia), requiring CDs-hydrogels to perform multifunctional roles. Further exploration of the interaction mechanisms between CDs-hydrogels and wound tissues, along with novel therapeutic strategies, is essential to enhance efficacy.
4.
Future research should focus on developing novel CDs-hydrogels that utilize the fluorescence/electrochemical signals of CDs for real-time monitoring of wound healing progression (e.g., inflammation regression, angiogenesis), thereby constructing a "monitoring-feedback-treatment" closed-loop system.
5.
Current research on CDs-hydrogels remains largely confined to preclinical stages. To advance clinical translation, rigorous clinical trials must evaluate their safety and effectiveness in human wound healing.
6.
CDs-hydrogels offer unique antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and regenerative capabilities, making their multifunctional combinations suitable for various types of wounds. Therefore, personalized customization for different customers and markets is becoming a trend in the future development of CDs-hydrogels.
7.
Although CDs-hydrogels have broad prospects for development, their commercialization still faces the following challenges. In large-scale production, ensuring the consistency of CD synthesis batches (including size, composition, and functional groups) remains a key issue to be resolved. Similarly, the synthesis of CDs involves multiple reaction steps, and the precise control of multi-step reaction conditions is difficult, making large-scale production challenging. Therefore, it is necessary to establish standardized protocols for CD synthesis in the future, with precise control of reaction conditions and raw material specifications to ensure batch reproducibility. In clinical applications, the crosslinkers used to manufacture CDs-hydrogels may exhibit potential biotoxicity, which needs to be carefully evaluated. Variations in individual immune responses may lead to adverse reactions or suboptimal therapeutic outcomes. Therefore, it is essential to conduct extensive immunocompatibility studies across diverse populations. Additionally, ensuring the sterility of CDs-hydrogels while maintaining their functional properties is a significant technical challenge. Traditional sterilization methods may alter their chemical structure or performance, thus there is an urgent need to develop alternative sterilization methods.
8.
To promote CDs-hydrogels as wound dressing products, it is necessary to establish relevant standards and policies. Research institutions and enterprises need to closely collaborate with regulatory authorities and actively participate in the development of regulatory standards to ensure that their research and applications comply with legal requirements. For ease of review, the relevant content has been highlighted in red in the revised manuscript.
